clean_story:
Focused attention. Altered perception.

The word hypnosis comes from Hypnos, the Greek god of sleep—but it’s not sleep. It’s a state where your mind narrows its focus like a flashlight beam, blocking out distractions. Imagine staring at a spinning coin until the world around it blurs. That’s the start.

A hypnotist’s voice acts like a metronome for your thoughts, guiding them into a rhythm. Your brain waves slow slightly, shifting from alert beta to relaxed alpha—the same shift as daydreaming. Yet you’re not asleep: you can still hear, respond, or snap out of it instantly. Surgeons use hypnosis to reduce pain during operations; patients report feeling pressure but no agony. Dentists apply it to calm nerves without drugs.

The trick isn’t magic—it’s suggestion. A hypnotized person told "Your arm is light as a feather" may lift it effortlessly, not because the arm changed, but because their brain temporarily believes it has. fMRI scans show hypnosis quiets the brain’s "reality-check" area (the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex), letting suggestions slip in like a backstage pass.

Three related subjects are placebo effects, lucid dreaming techniques, and how expectation shapes pain.
